mentions.note: Down from 387, long knives out; Cramer sees value. Buy some at 303, more at 290, additional at 280. Great company, good entry opportunity.
Underperforming despite Occidental Petroleum, Chevron holdings; Buffett premium gone post-succession. Market moving without founder conviction; not participating in oil rally.
Showed class act when fund gates opened — employees funded redemptions with own money. Demonstrates conviction in portfolio quality; won't short or bet against.

FY25: 170% sales growth, $2.5B revenue, $620M EBITDA. PepsiCo portfolio (Celsius, Alani, Rockstar) scaling via distribution gains, expansion to mocktails/fizz-free, international (Australia F1, Spain). Down 20% on consumer weakness — buying opportunity.
Caller asked about PayPal; Cramer preferred Capital One Financial as better alternative stock in financial services.

Global photonics leader for data center optical networking; Nvidia $2B investment + multi-decade supply deal validates strategy. US manufacturing, indium phosphide expertise, multi-year runway despite 40% YTD move.
High-speed Ethernet connectivity for data center; still a winner but Cramer prefers others (Marvell) with better positioning. Customer concentration, insider trading risks noted.
Substantial Berkshire position; geopolitical oil volatility limiting upside; Berkshire stock not participating in energy strength.
Chemical stock alongside Lyondell — both working well in current environment. Two-for-one recommendation.
Caller up 80% over 4 years; Cramer advises trim half given oil/gas sector volatility and need to lock in gains.
Polyolefins (polyethylene) manufacturer; chemical play working well. Cramer gives thumbs up, home run.

Post-diabetes spinoff focus on cardiovascular (accelerating), neuroscience, surgical divisions. Cheap valuation (16x FY26, <15x FY27), 3.2% yield, recession-proof profile, mid-to-high single-digit growth. Cramer most bullish in ages.
IPO priced $20, opened $19.05, trading $18; GLP-1 competition killed growth story. Cramer not interested; Medtronic retains 90%, negative overhang until year-end distribution.
Memory chip shortage supports near-term demand; part of data center semiconductor buying wave.
Strategic $2B investment in Coherent validates optical networking importance; customer and supplier relationship demonstrates ecosystem strength.
Highest-risk private credit firm; can't short because if loans truly solid they can sell at par (95+) to meet redemptions. Must prove portfolio quality; silence invites shorting. Don't buy stock.
Major Berkshire holding; oil volatility (Iran strikes, tanker narratives, +12% down day) creating whipsaw; not benefiting Berkshire stock despite strong holdings.
Just downgraded but Cramer stays long; upgraded because it's up so much, not due to fundamentals. Good company, hold position.
Down 50% from highs — Cramer was bullish, surprised at decline. Flagging for deeper workup; industry still liked, specific company weakness concerning.
Enrique Lores (ex-HPE CEO) now leading; company too hard to fix despite leadership change. Capital One Financial preferred alternative.
Down 50% from highs, wild trader; Cramer likes CEO Steve Huffman execution, terrific product, solid buy despite volatility.
Memory chip shortage driving data center demand; part of buzzsaw of buys alongside Western Digital, Micron offsetting geopolitical bearishness.
Medicare Advantage rates renegotiating; Centene's $7 drop shows volatility risk. Too topsy-turvy despite Optum strength. Cramer avoiding.
Memory shortage tailwind persists per HPE CEO; data center demand supports semiconductor suppliers.
Went private 2022 for $10.2B; natural AI bear case for private credit holders. But firm adjusting to new world — harder to short debt than stock; needs real defaults.
